metadata.title: Unveiling Privacy, Memorization, and Input Curvature Links

sections.title: Motivation and Background
content: Images from ImageNet ranked using input loss curvature. Input loss curvature was obtained using a single ResNet18 trained on ImageNet. Ten lowest curvature samples (top) and ten highest curvature samples (bottom) from the training set are visualized for 5 classes. Low curvature samples are 'prototypical' of their class, while high curvature samples are rare, difficult, and more likely memorized instances.

figures.description: 低输入曲率样本展示（鸟类图片）
高输入曲率样本展示（鸟类图片）

sections.title: Theory
content: Our theoretical framework provides upper bounds in Theorems 5.1, 5.3, and 5.4. These are visualized as links between Differential Privacy, Memorization, and Input Loss Curvature.

figures.description: 隐私、记忆与输入曲率之间的理论联系示意图

sections.title: Theoretical Results
content: Input Curvature is given by:
Curv_φ(z_i, S) = tr(H) = tr(∇²_x l(h_θ, z_i))
Theorem 5.1Input Curvature Upper Bounds Memorization
Lemma 5.2Privacy ⇒ Stability
Theorem 5.3Privacy ⇒ Low Input Loss Curvature
Theorem 5.4Privacy ⇒ Less Memorization

sections.title: Empirical Results
content: Memorization vs. Input Curvature
We calculate curvature scores by averaging over many seeds at the end of training. This measurement is proportional to the expected curvature score in Theorem 5.1.
Differential Privacy vs. Input Curvature
Differential Privacy vs. Memorization

ImageNet数据集上曲率与记忆化得分的关系图
隐私预算与曲率的关系图
隐私预算与记忆化的关系图

sections.title: Summary
content: We explore the theoretical link between memorization, curvature, and privacy.
Theoretical analysis is based on assumptions of stability, generalization, and Lipschitzness, applicable to non-convex settings like DNNs.
Main result: Memorization is upper-bounded by the curvature of the loss with respect to input and privacy.
Privacy bounds input curvature and memorization.
Theory validated using DNNs on CIFAR100 and ImageNet, showing a strong match between theoretical predictions and empirical results.

sections.title: MIA Perspective
content: A common approach to measure privacy is via membership inference attack (MIA). We used the LiRA (Carlini et al., 2022). More memorized samples are easier to detect using MIA attacks. For ImageNet MIA performance depends on the accuracy of the samples in the trainset.

figures.description: ImageNet数据集上的MIA攻击性能图
CIFAR100数据集上的MIA攻击性能图

sections.title: References
content: Abadi, M., Chu, A., Goodfellow, I., McMahan, H. B., Mironov, I., Talwar, K., and Zhang, L. Deep learning with differential privacy. In Proceedings of the 2016 ACM SIGSAC conference on computer and communications security, pp. 308-318, 2016.
Feldman, V. Does learning require memorization? a short tale about a long tail. arXiv preprint arXiv:1906.05271, 2019.

Nasr, M., Song, S., Thakurta, A., Papernot, N., and Carlini, N. Adversary instantiation: Lower bounds for differentially private machine learning. In 2021 IEEE Symposium on security and privacy (SP), pp. 866-882. IEEE, 2021.
